subject: This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI — Date of April 29, 2026
body:
This Week in RWE, Causal Inference & AI

Five papers worth your time at the intersection of real-world evidence, causal inference, and artificial intelligence.

Using Regression Discontinuity in Time to Strengthen Real-World Evidence: A Case Study in Lung Cancer

Chen et al. — Medical Decision Making (April 2026)

Propensity score methods in oncology RWE have a well-documented tendency to overestimate treatment benefits, and Chen et al. demonstrate exactly how much: ts-IPTW yields HR 0.52 for pembrolizumab versus docetaxel, while the long-run trial benchmark is HR 0.70 — a 35% overestimation. Regression discontinuity in time, exploiting pembrolizumab’s 2016 US approval as a natural threshold in 1,975 Flatiron Health patients, recovers HR 0.65 — 55% closer to the trial. Whenever a regulatory approval or guideline change creates a sharp discontinuity in treatment allocation, RDiT is the right causal tool and IPTW is the wrong one; this paper is the benchmark case study to cite.

Validation of an AI-Assisted Framework for Systematic Bias Assessment in Observational Studies

Etminan et al. — medRxiv (April 2026)

The failure mode of peer review for observational studies is well-known: reviewers rarely have time or domain expertise to systematically identify immortal time bias, confounding by indication, or selection bias across the full methodological landscape of a paper. EpiVise, Etminan and colleagues’ AI-assisted rule-based framework, achieves weighted kappa=0.75 against expert pharmacoepidemiologists on published studies and 0.77 on synthetic scenarios — substantial agreement by any standard. The real use case is scalable screening: EpiVise can process the volume of observational RWE submissions that regulators, HTA bodies, and journals face, flagging methodological red flags before they become published misconceptions.

Applications of Natural Language Processing and Large Language Models for Social Determinants of Health: Systematic Review

Rajwal et al. — Journal of Medical Internet Research (April 2026)

Social determinants of health are among the largest unmeasured confounders in pharmacoepidemiology, and this systematic review of 142 NLP/LLM studies documents both the rapid progress and the striking gaps. LLMs appear in only 19.7% of studies despite demonstrating strong performance (median F1 0.75–0.85), and structural SDOH factors like immigration status are almost completely absent (3.5% of studies). The reproducibility finding is the one that should anchor every RWE research agenda: only 49 studies shared code, fewer than half described model interpretability — meaning most of the literature on extracting key confounders from clinical text cannot be verified or replicated.

An Assessment of the Real-World Data Platform TriNetX for Measuring the Association Between Group A Streptococcus and Neuropsychiatric Diagnoses

Gao et al. — medRxiv (April 2026)

Federated data networks like TriNetX are being deployed for increasingly consequential questions, yet their limitations in detecting specific epidemiological signals are rarely stress-tested against known ground truth. Gao and Frankovich et al. run exactly that test: 178,301 propensity-score-matched patients in TriNetX, asking whether the platform can recover known GAS post-infectious associations (ARF, PSRA, PSGN). It largely cannot — not because the design is wrong, but because these conditions are too rare, heterogeneous, and diagnostically undercoded in administrative data. This is the honest platform-limitation paper that every team planning to study rare post-infectious or post-treatment phenotypes in federated EHR data should read before interpreting null results.

AgentClinic: a multimodal benchmark for tool-using clinical AI agents

Schmidgall et al. — NPJ Digital Medicine (April 2026)

Every LLM clinical benchmark that uses multiple-choice QA is measuring a different capability than what clinical AI systems actually need. AgentClinic makes this concrete: when MedQA problems are reframed as sequential decision-making with patient interactions, tool use, and incomplete information, diagnostic accuracy drops to below a tenth of original scores for some models. Claude-3.5 leads overall in nine specialties and seven languages, but the methodologically important finding is the 92% relative improvement Llama-3 achieves with a persistent notebook tool — tool architecture matters as much as base model capability for real-world clinical deployment.
